goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena! You're feeling great this morning with a 93/100 score and high energy — that's a strong start.

Last night you got **8.2 hours** of sleep with excellent efficiency (92.8%) — that's over an hour more than your usual and your best sleep metrics in a while. Your body had solid recovery time. Yesterday, though, tells a different story: your stress was high with low coping capacity (48/100), and your energy and focus scores dropped sharply to 25 and 10. You still managed 14k steps and 86 minutes of MVPA, which is impressive given how drained you felt mentally.

Here's what stands out: your HRV has been declining while stress stays elevated — that's your nervous system telling you it's carrying a load. And despite eating late (~10:25 PM) and having caffeine after 2pm yesterday, you slept well objectively, though that pattern can make sleep perception inconsistent over time. Your protein intake was also lower than usual at 70.8g, which might not be fueling you enough for your active days.

Today is about **bridging that gap** between how you feel this morning and what your body's stress signals suggest. The goal is to sustain this good energy while giving your system what it needs to handle yesterday's accumulated strain.

We're focusing on stress management, nutrition support, and movement stability to help your body catch up with your positive morning mindset.

Let's use today's strong start to build a more balanced day that supports both your energy and recovery.

assumptions: High overall score (93) suggests subjective wellbeing is strong despite declining mood/focus/energy scores
Missing journal data limits context for emotional state
Low nutrition confidence (0.48) means food data has moderate estimation error
Sleep duration (8.15h) meets goal target, focus should shift to consistency and perception
Physio proxy score (59) significantly lower than overall score (93) indicates potential subjective-objective mismatch
soft_constraints: prioritize sleep perception improvement alongside duration
consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend

generated_actions.title: When you feel overwhelmed, take a 2-minute breathing reset
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's stress score was volatile and coping capacity was low (48, well below baseline). With today's 'high workload' hard constraint, an immediate, tiny breathing reset can help manage stress spikes without disrupting your schedule.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete a breathing reset when you felt overwhelmed today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the breathing exercise when they felt overwhelmed

assumptions: You can recognize moments of overwhelm
generated_actions.description: If you notice tension building during your tight schedule today, pause for exactly 2 minutes. Close your eyes and breathe slowly: inhale 4 seconds, hold 4, exhale 6. Repeat 5 times.

generated_actions.title: Add a protein-rich snack to your afternoon routine
generated_actions.rationale: Protein intake is declining (-7.74 slope) and below baseline (94.01g). Yesterday's meals were carb-heavy (mixed berry juice, potato chips, chocolate). Adding a protein snack addresses the nutrition gap risk flag and may help stabilize energy, which was critically low yesterday (25).

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 85g

assumptions: You have access to a protein-rich snack
generated_actions.description: Between lunch and dinner, when you typically feel an energy dip, eat a handful of nuts, a yogurt, or a protein bar. Yesterday's protein intake (70.8g) was below baseline.

generated_actions.title: Schedule a 10-minute walk after lunch to break sedentary time
generated_actions.rationale: Sedentary minutes are trending upward (+29.94 slope) despite high step count yesterday (14k). A scheduled post-lunch walk addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' and can help stabilize mood and focus, which are declining sharply.

evaluation.success_definition: Sedentary minutes today are less than or equal to 700

assumptions: You have flexibility after lunch
generated_actions.description: After you finish lunch, take a 10-minute walk outside or around your building. This counters the sedentary trend (sedentary minutes increasing +29.94 slope) and provides a mental reset.

generated_actions.title: Finish eating by 9:00 PM and avoid caffeine after 2pm
generated_actions.rationale: Eating window is trending later (+2.75 slope) and yesterday included caffeine after 2pm. Despite excellent objective sleep metrics, this habit could affect sleep perception and consistency. Today's priority is 'sleep_consistency_recovery' and a soft constraint is 'maintain earlier eating window (<1200 min)'.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you avoid caffeine after 2pm today?
evaluation.success_definition: Last meal occurs before 9:00 PM (time_last_meal_minutes < 1140) AND user reports no caffeine after 2pm

assumptions: You can control evening meal timing and caffeine intake
generated_actions.description: Given yesterday's late meal (~10:25 PM) and caffeine after 2pm, set a clear cutoff tonight: eat your last meal by 9:00 PM and avoid any caffeine after 2pm today.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Stop eating by 9:30 PM to protect your wind-down routine
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's eating window was 882 minutes with a very late last meal (1370 minutes/~10:50 PM), and the trend is positive (+12 slope). Today's priority is 'sleep_consistency' and a soft constraint is 'maintain earlier eating window (<1200 min)'. Closing the window earlier supports sleep hygiene and addresses the trend.

evaluation.success_definition: Your last recorded meal occurs before 9:30 PM (time_last_meal_minutes < 1170)

assumptions: You have control over evening meal timing
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Given yesterday's late meals (~10:50 PM) and today's priority for sleep consistency, set a clear cutoff: finish any eating by 9:30 PM tonight. This creates space for your evening wind-down.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Do a 2-minute breathing reset after your basketball practice
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's stress was elevated (71) and trending upward steeply (+12.5 slope), despite high coping (83). With a 'high workload' hard constraint today, anchoring a stress-management habit to your existing morning routine (practice) helps prevent accumulation.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the breathing reset after your practice?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the breathing exercise after basketball practice

assumptions: You finish practice around 7am
You can take 2 minutes before work
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you finish your 6am basketball practice, take 2 minutes before starting work. Sit quietly and do 5 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing (inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s) to transition from physical activity to mental workload.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Add a protein source to your lunch meal today
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's protein was 68g, below fast baseline (101.75) and trending downward (-11.25 slope). Lunch description was '2 falafel pockets'. Adding protein addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and the soft constraint 'nutrition logging accuracy improvement needed' by making meals more substantial and easier to log accurately.

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 80g

assumptions: You have control over lunch composition
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When preparing or ordering lunch, intentionally include a protein-rich item (e.g., chicken, eggs, lentils, yogurt). Yesterday's lunch was '2 falafel pockets' and protein intake was moderate (68g), below baseline. This small addition supports energy and nutrition confidence.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Write down one positive sleep thought during your wind-down
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: There's a significant 'sleep perception gap': objective metrics were strong yesterday (92.91% efficiency, 6.85% wake ratio) but subjective sleep quality rating was poor (37). A simple positive reflection before bed can help bridge this gap and improve subjective experience, supporting 'sleep_consistency'.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you write down a positive sleep thought before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the written reflection

assumptions: You have pen and paper available
previous_day_generated_actions.description: During your evening wind-down routine (e.g., after brushing teeth), write one sentence on paper about something you appreciate about your sleep or a hopeful thought for tonight's rest. This addresses the perception gap.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena!

You're starting today with a perfect 100 score and high energy — that's fantastic to hear. Let's look at what's behind that feeling.

First, sleep: last night you got **6.3 hours** with excellent efficiency (92.9%) and very little wake time. That's solid quality sleep, even though it's a bit shorter than your usual. What's interesting is the gap between what your body experienced last night and how you *felt* about sleep yesterday — your subjective sleep quality rating from yesterday was only 37, while last night's objective metrics were strong. This perception-reality disconnect is something we see when stress is high, which brings us to yesterday's other big signal: your stress score hit **71**, well above your baseline and trending upward steeply. Despite that elevated stress, your coping capacity was remarkably high at 83, and your HRV recovery metric stayed excellent at 86.3. This tells me you're managing stress effectively on a physiological level, even when you're feeling it subjectively.

Yesterday was also notably low on movement — just 6,529 steps and no moderate activity — which usually happens when you're conserving energy during high-stress periods. Your nutrition showed reduced intake (1,715 calories, 68g protein) with a late dinner around 10:50 PM. The good news is that late eating didn't disrupt your sleep efficiency last night, and your social score was outstanding at 88 — a real strength right now.

Today's theme is about **protecting your recovery momentum** while addressing the stress and perception gaps that showed up yesterday. You've got the physiological foundation (great HRV, good sleep efficiency) — now it's about aligning your experience with that foundation.

Your social energy has been consistently high all week — lean into that strength today while giving your body the intentional recovery it deserves after yesterday's stress load.

evidence_quotes: I worked the whole day but didn't actually get anything I needed to get done

meal_descriptions.description: 2 chicken sausages and a hasbrown. Avocado toast with 2 fried eggs.
Mixed greens and fired chicken salad
Mixed berry juice and some potato chips
